key_questions: Number of lesions and extent of involvement?
Contagious setting: school/daycare, family members affected?
Underlying skin condition (eczema — common portal of entry)?
red_flags: Rapidly spreading or worsening despite treatment → MRSA, cellulitis
Bullous impetigo in infant — more serious (systemic S. aureus)
Post-strep glomerulonephritis risk (hematuria, edema) — with nephritogenic strains

first_line.name: Topical Antibiotic (localized)
first_line.drugs: Mupirocin 2% ointment (Bactroban) TID × 5 days | Fusidic acid 2% cream (Fucidin) TID × 5 days
first_line.notes: Topical therapy equivalent to oral for localized non-bullous impetigo (≤5 lesions). Reduces systemic antibiotic exposure and resistance risk. Pharmacist can prescribe under Ontario minor ailments. Clean crusts with warm water before applying. Both mupirocin and fusidic acid cover S. aureus and Group A Strep.

second_line.name: Oral Antibiotic (widespread/bullous)
second_line.drugs: Cephalexin 500 mg QID × 7 days (children: 25 mg/kg/day ÷ QID) | Amoxicillin-clavulanate 500/125 mg TID × 7 days
second_line.notes: For widespread impetigo (>5 lesions), bullous type, or treatment failure with topical. Cephalexin covers S. aureus (MSSA) and Strep. If MRSA suspected (no response to cephalexin): TMP-SMX DS BID × 7 days.

non_pharm: Keep lesions covered with gauze to prevent spread
Child should stay home from school/daycare until 24–48h after starting antibiotic AND lesions crusted
Do not share towels, washcloths, or bedding
Trim nails to prevent scratching and autoinoculation
treatment.refer_when: Widespread impetigo; bullous impetigo in infant; systemic symptoms (fever); failure to improve after 48–72h; facial impetigo near eyes; suspected MRSA; immunocompromised patient
